cataloging_subject.label: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English poem, not any particular manuscript, scholarly edition, digital encoding, translation, or modern adaptation.

catalog_summary.title: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous (Gawain Poet)
catalog_summary.editor: J. R. R. Tolkien and E. V. Gordon; revised by Norman Davis
catalog_summary.region: Staffordshire, England (poet's dialect); unique witness copied in the North-West Midlands

identifiers.scheme: A New Index of Middle English Verse

titles.value: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight

assertion.notes: Confirmed as the established preferred title; the unique manuscript does not supply this title as an authorial heading.

titles.value: Siþen þe sege and þe assaut watz sesed at Troye

assertion.notes: The conventional label Gawain Poet identifies an unknown author; it is not a recovered personal name.
This is a scholarly work-level dating, not the date 1400 supplied in the legacy XML profile header and not the full catalogue date range for the physical codex.

notes: The copying date is evidence for attestation and a terminus for composition; it is not automatically the composition date.

places.label: South-East Cheshire or North-East Staffordshire, England

assertion.notes: The British Library distinguishes the poet's dialect, thought to lie farther south in Staffordshire, from the unique witness's scribal dialect.

notes: Dialect localization does not prove an exact place of composition or the author's residence.
This statement localizes the language of the sole surviving copy, not necessarily the author's original spelling system.

language_statements.label: Anglo-Norman French

scope.description: The closing motto HONY SOYT QUI MAL PENCE

notes: The motto is separately attested in the encoded text; it does not make French a co-primary language of the poem.

form_statements.description: Alliterative long-line verse arranged in variable-length stanza groups ending in a rhymed bob and wheel.

summaries.value: At Arthur's New Year feast, a supernatural green knight proposes a beheading exchange. Gawain accepts, strikes the first blow, and a year later journeys to keep the return appointment. At Hautdesert his truth is tested through an exchange-of-winnings pact and three bedroom temptations; he conceals a supposedly protective green girdle. The Green Knight reveals the tests at the Green Chapel, and Gawain returns to Camelot chastened by the gap between chivalric ideals and human conduct.

The poem survives uniquely in British Library Cotton MS Nero A X/2 with Pearl, Cleanness, and Patience. The repository text is not a diplomatic transcription of the manuscript: it descends through Tolkien and Gordon's edition as revised by Norman Davis and a U-M/OTA electronic encoding that omits the print edition's front and back matter, notes, and illustrations.

extent.description: The repository XML contains four passus, 100 LG verse-group elements, and 2,533 L elements. The encoded group numbering runs 1–101 but omits a separate LG numbered 14, and XML line elements do not map one-for-one to the scholarly database's 2,530-line count; these are encoding metrics, not a silent emendation of the poem.

content_parts.label: Passus I
content_parts.summary: Arthur's New Year feast, the Green Knight's beheading challenge, Gawain's acceptance and blow, and the covenant to receive a return stroke after a year and a day.

content_parts.label: Passus II
content_parts.summary: Gawain arms and departs, crosses the wintry wilderness, reaches Hautdesert for Christmas, and agrees to exchange his gains with the lord of the castle.

content_parts.label: Passus III
content_parts.summary: Three hunts alternate with three bedroom temptations. Gawain returns kisses under the exchange pact but accepts and conceals the lady's green girdle on the third day.

content_parts.label: Passus IV
content_parts.summary: At the Green Chapel the third feigned blow nicks Gawain. The Green Knight reveals the linked tests, Gawain confesses his fault, and Camelot adopts the green girdle when he returns.

related_works.title: Pearl
notes: Pearl shares the unique manuscript, dialectal and formal affinities, and the conventional attribution to the same unknown poet; common authorship is scholarly attribution rather than a named-author fact.

evidence.citation: Gawain XML header evidence in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: The local header identifies the title, anonymous authorship, U-M and OTA identifiers, 1993 distribution, the Tolkien-Gordon 1967 second edition, and omitted print matter.

evidence.citation: Gawain XML content evidence in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: The complete local encoded poem supports the incipit, language, four-part narrative summary, older foliation, closing motto, and content descriptions.

evidence.citation: Gawain XML structural inspection evidence in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: XPath counts support four passus, 100 encoded verse groups, 2,533 encoded line elements, and the per-passus metrics recorded here.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, ‘Sir Gawain and the Green Knight,’ Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item Gawain.
evidence.summary: U-M identifies the anonymous work, Oxford: Clarendon Press 1967 source, OTA number U*-62-A, four passus, public web text, and No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Nero A X/2, record 040-003316720.
evidence.locator: Scope and Content; Digitised Content; Physical Characteristics; Origin
evidence.summary: The BL record identifies the four anonymous poems, Gawain at modern folios 94v–130r, copying by one scribe between 1375 and 1400, the scribal and poet dialect localizations, parchment codex, current access conditions, and twelve drawings.

evidence.citation: West Virginia University Press, description of Larry D. Benson and Daniel Donoghue, Sir Gawain and the Green Knight (2012).
evidence.summary: The university press describes the work as a late-fourteenth-century Middle English alliterative romance concerning Gawain's challenge, oath, chivalry, loyalty, and honor.

evidence.citation: University of York, Database of Middle English Romance, ‘London, British Library, MS Cotton Nero A.x/2.’
evidence.summary: The scholarly database identifies Gawain as a unique copy at folios 94v–130r, NIMEV 3144, containing 2,530 lines; it dates and localizes the manuscript to the late-fourteenth-century North-West Midlands.

evidence.citation: Ad Putter and Myra Stokes, ‘The Gawain Poet,’ Oxford Bibliographies in Medieval Studies.
evidence.summary: The scholarly overview treats the anonymous Gawain Poet corpus, its Northwest Midland Middle English, and the shared formal and thematic features of the four Cotton Nero A X/2 poems.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated more narrowly than the late fourteenth century without conflating composition with copying or illustration dates?
next_steps: Review current specialist arguments based on historical allusions, vocabulary, meter, and manuscript chronology.
Preserve competing datings as separate assertions rather than replacing the conservative range without explicit evidence.

open_questions.question: What confidence should the catalog assign to common authorship of Gawain, Pearl, Cleanness, and Patience?
next_steps: Review current authorship studies and distinguish shared scribal transmission from authorial attribution.
Retain Anonymous as the responsibility unless a stronger consensus statement is documented.

notes: The legacy XML profile date 1400 is retained as lineage evidence but is not treated as an exact composition date.
The catalog distinguishes the poem, the sole manuscript witness, the 1967 source edition, the U-M/OTA digital encoding, and the checked-in repository artifact.
The XML's older foliation and the BL catalogue's modern foliation are both recorded in lineage; neither has been silently converted into the other.
